ai_literacy.title: AI Literacy
ai_literacy.subtitle: Understanding AI for Research Administration
ai_literacy.description: Welcome to the Vandal Workflow Architect certification. By the end of this program, you'll earn an official VWA credential recognizing your ability to design, validate, and deploy agentic AI workflows for research administration. This first module builds your foundation: what LLMs actually do, and how Vandalizer 5.0 combines conversational chat with validated workflows, quality scores, and source-linked answers to make AI trustworthy for professional RA work. No technical skills required yet.
objectives: Understand what an LLM is and how it generates text
Learn the key terms you'll encounter throughout this certification
Reflect on your own experience and comfort level with AI tools
tips: There are no wrong answers on the self-assessment; it's for your own reflection
The key terms in this module will come up repeatedly in later modules
If you're skeptical about AI, that's healthy. This module is designed to give you an honest picture

lessons.title: What is an LLM, really?
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to explain why LLMs make mistakes and why they're still useful.
lessons.content:
A Large Language Model (LLM) is not thinking. It is not sentient. It does not understand your documents the way you do.

An LLM is a sophisticated pattern-completion engine trained on vast amounts of text. When you give it a prompt, it predicts the most likely next words based on patterns it learned during training. This is both why it's capable and why it makes mistakes.

It's capable because human language follows patterns. A grant proposal has a predictable structure: PI name, institution, budget, aims. The LLM has seen thousands of similar documents and can reliably identify these patterns.

It makes mistakes because pattern-matching is not understanding. The LLM doesn't know what a budget is. It knows that numbers near the word "budget" are likely dollar amounts. When the pattern breaks (unusual formatting, ambiguous language), the LLM may confidently produce wrong answers.

options.text: Thinking through the problem logically, like a human would
options.explanation: LLMs don't "think". They predict patterns based on training data.

options.text: Predicting the most likely next words based on patterns from training data
options.explanation: Correct! LLMs are pattern-completion engines trained on vast text.

options.text: Looking up answers in a database of facts
options.explanation: LLMs don't have a database; they learned patterns from training text.

options.text: Running a search engine to find relevant information
options.explanation: LLMs generate text from learned patterns, not from searching the internet.

lessons.title: What AI is genuinely good at
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know which research admin tasks are best suited for AI automation.
lessons.content:
AI excels at tasks that involve pattern recognition across large volumes of text:

• **Extracting specific information** from long documents — Finding the PI name, budget, and project period in a 50-page proposal.
• **Summarizing** — Condensing a progress report into key findings and milestones.
• **Comparing across documents** — Identifying differences between two versions of a budget justification.
• **Drafting routine text** — Generating first drafts of compliance summaries or progress report templates.
• **Processing many documents consistently** — Applying the same extraction to 200 proposals and getting results in the same format every time.

The common thread: these are tasks where a human would be doing repetitive reading and data entry. AI handles the volume; you handle the judgment.

lessons.title: What AI is genuinely bad at
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to identify AI limitations and avoid common pitfalls in research administration.
lessons.content:
Honesty about AI's limitations is essential for responsible use in research administration:

• **Judgment calls requiring institutional knowledge** — AI doesn't know your university's internal policies, political dynamics, risk tolerance, or historical context.
• **Catching its own mistakes** — An LLM cannot reliably self-check. If it extracts the wrong budget figure, it won't flag the error. That's your job.
• **Math** — LLMs frequently make arithmetic errors. Never trust an LLM to add up budget line items. Use code execution nodes for calculations.
• **Novel or unusual document formats** — If a document doesn't follow standard patterns (hand-written notes, unusual layouts, scanned images with poor OCR), extraction quality drops significantly.
• **Replacing professional judgment on compliance** — AI can flag potential issues, but determining whether a proposal actually meets regulatory requirements requires your expertise.

The pattern: AI is a powerful first-pass tool. It does the reading; you do the thinking.

knowledge_check.question: Which task is AI worst at?

options.text: Extracting PI names from grant proposals
options.explanation: This is actually a strong suit for AI — it's pattern-based extraction from structured documents.

options.text: Summarizing progress reports
options.explanation: Summarization is one of AI's strengths — it's good at condensing text.

options.text: Making judgment calls that require institutional knowledge
options.explanation: Correct! AI doesn't know your institution's policies, politics, or historical context. That requires your expertise.

options.text: Processing 200 documents in the same format
options.explanation: Batch processing with consistent format is ideal for AI — it handles repetition well.

lessons.title: AI for research administration
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to describe where AI fits in common research admin workflows and where it doesn't.
lessons.content:
Here's what AI-assisted research administration looks like in practice:

• **Proposal intake** — Upload 30 new proposals to a secure, institutionally managed AI environment. A workflow extracts PI name, agency, budget, and key dates from each one in minutes instead of hours — without exposing sensitive proposal data to consumer AI tools or free-tier services.
• **Progress report processing** — Extract accomplishments, publications, and expenditures from annual reports. Flag any that are missing required sections.
• **Compliance pre-screening** — Check proposals against a list of required elements (human subjects approval, data management plan, budget justification) and flag gaps.
• **Subaward review** — Extract parties, amounts, and terms from subaward agreements. Compare against institutional templates.

The pattern in every case: AI does the first pass of extraction, you do the second pass of verification and judgment. The AI handles volume and consistency. You bring expertise and accountability.

lessons.title: From generic chat to validated agentic chat
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand why Vandalizer's chat is different from ChatGPT or Copilot, and how it turns conversation into a professional workflow system.
lessons.content:
You may have used ChatGPT or Copilot to ask questions about a document. It's convenient, but for professional research administration work the generic experience falls short:

• **Inconsistent format** — Ask the same question twice and you get differently structured answers.
• **No audit trail** — There's no record of what was extracted, when, or from which document.
• **Can't scale** — You shouldn't paste 200 proposals into a consumer chatbot one at a time.
• **No verification** — There's no systematic way to check whether the answers are correct.
• **No institutional grounding** — The model doesn't know your policies, your OSP handbook, or your team's proven processes.

Vandalizer 5.0 takes the same conversational interface you're already comfortable with and puts a trust layer underneath it. The chat can still answer ad-hoc questions — but when it matters, it drives **validated workflows**, **verified extractions**, and **sourced knowledge bases** on your behalf:

• **Every tool call is logged.** Ask the agent to run an extraction and you see the exact tool, inputs, and outputs — captured in the audit log.
• **Every result carries a quality score.** Accuracy, consistency, and the number of test cases behind a template all surface inline. You can tell at a glance whether an answer is ready to act on.
• **Writes require confirmation.** Creating a knowledge base or dispatching a workflow pauses for a preview step before anything changes.
• **Answers cite their sources.** Knowledge-base replies show the document passages they used; click any passage to jump to the source.
• **The system gets smarter with use.** Turn a good extraction into a test case in one click. The more your team verifies, the higher the quality tier climbs.

This is the bridge from "AI as a toy" to "AI as a professional tool." The rest of this certification teaches you to design the validated workflows the agent calls, verify their accuracy, and deploy them across your office — so the chat your team uses every day is backed by trust signals no generic AI can offer. When you complete all 11 modules, you'll earn your Vandal Workflow Architect certification.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
LLM (Large Language Model) — You've now seen how LLMs are used to extract structured fields from grant proposals and summarize documents. The key: an LLM is a pattern-completion engine, not a thinking machine. It predicts likely text based on training patterns. When you build a workflow step, you're giving it patterns to match — the clearer your field names and prompts, the better the results.

Prompt — The instructions you give an LLM. You'll write many of these as you build workflows. Quality matters: "Extract the PI name from the proposal cover page" outperforms "find the PI."

Hallucination — When an LLM generates a plausible-sounding value that isn't actually in the document. The #1 risk in research administration AI use. It's why you'll learn to build validation plans in Module 8.

Structured Output — The mechanism that makes batch processing possible. Instead of free-form text, the LLM is constrained to return JSON matching your field definitions — same fields, same format, every document, every time.

Token — The unit LLMs work in. Roughly ¾ of a word. Relevant when documents are very long — models have context limits that affect how much text they can process at once.

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — How the chat feature works. Relevant document excerpts are retrieved and provided to the LLM before it answers. This grounds responses in your actual documents rather than the LLM's training data.

assessment.title: Self-Assessment
assessment.subtitle: There are no wrong answers. This is about understanding your starting point.

questions.question: Which best describes your experience with AI tools?
options: I have not used any AI tools
I've tried a chatbot (ChatGPT, Copilot, etc.) a few times
I use AI tools occasionally for work or personal tasks
I use AI tools regularly and feel confident with them

questions.question: When you think about using AI in research administration, how do you feel?
options: Skeptical — I worry about accuracy and whether it's appropriate for my work
Cautious but curious — I want to understand it before forming an opinion
Open to it if someone shows me how it helps with tasks I already do
Enthusiastic — I'm looking for ways to integrate AI into my workflows

questions.question: What is your biggest concern about AI for research administration?
options: That it will make mistakes I won't catch, especially with compliance or financial data
That it's a black box — I won't understand how it reaches its answers
That it will change my role in ways I'm not comfortable with
I don't have major concerns, but I want to learn the right way to use it

foundations.title: Foundations
foundations.subtitle: Documents In, Intelligence Out
foundations.description: Learn the basics of workflows using a sample NSF proposal from Dr. Sarah Chen. Click Set Up Lab to load it, then build your first extraction workflow.
objectives: Add the sample NSF proposal to your workspace
Create a workflow with an Extraction step and 5 fields
Run the workflow and verify extracted values
tips: The sample NSF proposal contains clearly labeled fields like PI Name, Institution, and Total Budget
Use clear, descriptive field names in your Extraction that match the document labels
After running, check that PI Name = Sarah Chen and Total Budget = $485,000

lessons.title: What is a workflow?
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand how workflows differ from ad-hoc chat and why that matters for research administration.
lessons.content: A workflow is a reusable pipeline that processes a document through a series of defined steps — build it once, run it against any document. Unlike ad-hoc chat, workflows produce consistent, structured output. That consistency is what lets you process 500 grant proposals the same way you process one.

lessons.title: What you'll build
lessons.content:
Here's the end state for this module's lab exercise:

• A workflow called something like "Grant Proposal Extractor"
• An Extraction with at least 5 fields: PI Name, Total Budget, Sponsoring Agency, Project Period, Institution
• A run result showing Dr. Sarah Chen extracted from the sample NSF proposal, with a budget of $485,000

The lessons that follow explain each piece. By the time you reach the lab, every step will be familiar.

lessons.title: The document pipeline
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand what happens to a document between upload and extraction.
lessons.content:
When you upload a document, Vandalizer processes it through several stages:

1. **Text extraction** — The raw text is pulled from PDFs, DOCX, XLSX, and HTML files using specialized readers.
2. **Chunking** — The text is split into overlapping segments for semantic search.
3. **Embedding** — Each chunk is embedded into ChromaDB, a vector database, so it can be searched semantically.

When a workflow runs an Extraction step, the LLM receives the full document text and your Extraction fields, and returns structured JSON with the extracted values.

lessons.title: Build your first workflow
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be ready to run your first extraction workflow on a real document.
lessons.content:
When you start the lab exercise, here's what you'll do:

1. Click **Set Up Lab** (the button at the top of this module) to load the sample NSF proposal into your workspace.
2. Go to the **Library** tab and click **New** to create a new workflow.
3. Give your workflow a clear name, like "Grant Proposal Extractor", and add a description such as "Extracts key details from grant proposals".
4. Add a step and give it a name, like "Extract Grant Details". Then add an Extraction task to that step.
5. In the Extraction task settings, select an existing Extraction or create a new one.
6. Add at least 3 fields to your Extraction — for example: "Principal Investigator", "Funding Amount", "Sponsoring Agency".
7. Select the sample NSF proposal, then click Run to execute the workflow.
8. Review the extracted results in the output panel.

lessons.title: Why structured extraction matters
lessons.content: Unstructured AI responses vary every time — different formatting, different phrasing, different levels of detail. Structured extraction forces the AI to return consistent, machine-readable JSON. This means you can reliably extract the same fields from hundreds of documents and compare, aggregate, or export the results. It turns documents from unstructured text into queryable data.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Workflow — You just built one. A saved pipeline that processes documents through a series of steps. The recipe analogy holds: define it once, run it against any document. The extraction workflow from this module is the foundation for everything that follows.

Step — One stage in your pipeline. Your first workflow had one step (extraction). Later modules add reasoning and delivery steps to create more powerful pipelines.

Task — The specific operation inside a step. The Extraction task you configured defined which fields the LLM should find and return as structured JSON.

Extraction — The collection of fields you defined. Think of it as your data dictionary — it tells the LLM exactly what to look for and what format to return it in.

Extract Key — Each individual field in your Extraction: "PI Name", "Total Budget", "Sponsoring Agency". Well-named extract keys get better results because the LLM uses the name as a clue about what to find.

process_mapping.title: Thinking in Workflows
process_mapping.subtitle: See Your Work as Automatable Processes
process_mapping.description: Before you can build a workflow, you need to see your work differently. This module teaches you to recognize the repeatable processes hiding in your daily tasks, identify which parts are suitable for AI, and which parts need your expertise.
objectives: Recognize repeatable processes in your research administration work
Identify which parts of a process are AI-suitable vs. human-judgment
Apply the process decomposition framework to a real task from your work
tips: Think about the tasks you do every week that follow the same pattern
The best workflow candidates are tasks where you spend most of your time reading and re-typing
Don't try to automate everything — the goal is to automate the tedious parts so you can focus on the important parts

lessons.title: From one workflow to many
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to identify repeatable processes in your daily work that are ready for automation.
lessons.content:
You've built a basic workflow. Now the question is: what else in your office should be a workflow?

Module 1 taught you the mechanics. This module gives you a framework for finding the processes worth automating. The pattern you're looking for: any task where you spend 80% of your time reading documents and 20% making decisions about what you found. The reading is automatable. The deciding stays yours.

By the end of this module, you'll have mapped a real process from your own work to a workflow architecture — not a hypothetical, but something you could actually build next.

lessons.title: Finding the repetition
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll have a practical test for identifying which processes to automate.
lessons.content:
The best candidates for workflows are tasks that share three properties:

1. **You do them repeatedly** — not once, but dozens or hundreds of times. Processing proposals, reviewing progress reports, checking compliance documents.

2. **You follow the same steps each time** — you look for the same information, in the same kinds of documents, and produce the same kind of output.

3. **Most of the time is spent reading, not thinking** — you spend 80% of your time finding information in documents and 20% making decisions about it.

Here's a quick test: could you write step-by-step instructions for a new hire to do this task? If yes, it's a workflow. If the instructions would be "use your judgment," that specific step stays human.

Common research admin processes that pass this test:
• Processing incoming proposals (extracting key fields)
• Reviewing progress reports (checking completeness)
• Pre-screening for compliance (finding required elements)
• Summarizing subaward terms (extracting parties and obligations)
• Preparing data for reports (gathering numbers from multiple documents)

knowledge_check.question: What makes a process a good candidate for AI automation?

options.text: It requires deep institutional knowledge and professional judgment
options.explanation: Tasks requiring judgment should stay with humans — AI handles the repetitive reading part.

options.text: It's repetitive, document-based, and most time is spent reading rather than thinking
options.explanation: Correct! The best AI candidates are repetitive tasks where you spend most time extracting information from documents.

options.text: It only happens once or twice a year
options.explanation: Rare tasks don't benefit much from automation — the setup cost outweighs the time saved.

options.text: It involves making complex financial decisions
options.explanation: Complex decisions require human expertise. AI is better at the extraction that feeds into those decisions.

lessons.title: The AI suitability test
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll have a decision framework for choosing between Extraction, Code Execution, and keeping a step human.
lessons.content:
Not every step in your process should be automated. Here's a practical framework:

**AI-suitable** (automate these):
• Reading a document and finding specific information → Extraction
• Summarizing or paraphrasing document content → Prompt
• Comparing information across documents → Prompt
• Reformatting data from one structure to another → Formatter
• Drafting routine text based on extracted data → Prompt

**Use code, not AI**:
• Adding up numbers or computing percentages → Code Execution
• Date calculations or comparisons → Code Execution
• Applying deterministic rules ("if budget > $500K, flag for review") → Code Execution

**Keep human**:
• Deciding whether something meets a policy requirement
• Interpreting ambiguous or unusual situations
• Making recommendations that require institutional context
• Anything where a mistake has serious consequences and can't be easily caught

The pattern: AI reads and extracts. Code computes. Humans judge.

lessons.title: Walkthrough: Mapping a real process
lessons.content:
Let's decompose "processing incoming proposals" step by step:

1. **Receive the proposal document** (PDF) → Input: this is your workflow trigger.

2. **Find PI name, institution, budget, project dates, agency** → AI-suitable: this is reading and extracting. Map to an Extraction step.

3. **Check whether required sections are present** (data management plan, budget justification, biosketches) → AI-suitable: checking for presence of sections. Map to a Prompt step.

4. **Verify the budget adds up** → Use code: map to a Code Execution step that sums extracted line items.

5. **Decide whether to flag for compliance review** → Keep human: this requires institutional judgment. This is where your workflow ends and your review begins.

6. **Enter data into your tracking system** → The workflow's structured output makes this copy-paste or even automated via API.

Result: a 3-step workflow (Extract → Check sections → Verify budget) that does 70% of the work, leaving you to do the 30% that requires your expertise.

lessons.title: Common processes that become workflows
lessons.content:
Here are the most common research administration processes and how they map to workflows:

• **Proposal intake** — Extract key fields, check completeness, flag gaps. 3-4 steps.
• **Progress report review** — Extract accomplishments, publications, expenditures. Summarize and compare to milestones. 3 steps.
• **Compliance pre-screening** — Extract required elements, check against a compliance checklist, produce a gap report. 3-4 steps.
• **Budget review** — Extract line items, compute totals, compare to limits, produce a summary. 4 steps.
• **Subaward processing** — Extract parties, amounts, terms, deliverables. Flag deviations from templates. 3 steps.
• **Award closeout** — Gather final expenditures, publications, and deliverables from multiple documents. 4-5 steps.

Notice the pattern: every workflow starts with extraction (getting data out of documents) and ends with either a human review point or a produced deliverable. The middle steps are where analysis, comparison, and computation happen.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Use these five elements to describe any process you're considering automating. You've seen them applied to proposal intake — now practice applying them to a process from your own work.

Input — What triggers this process? Usually a document arriving: a proposal, a report, a subaward agreement, a budget justification.

Steps — The discrete actions you take, in order. Each step has a clear purpose: "find the PI name," "check the budget," "write a summary."

Decision Points — Where you apply judgment: "Is this budget reasonable?" "Does this meet compliance requirements?" These are where human expertise is essential and AI steps back.

Handoffs — Where work passes between people: "Send to compliance officer for review," "Return to PI for corrections."

Output — What's produced at the end: a completed form, a summary report, a recommendation, data entered into a system.

assessment.title: Process Reflection
assessment.subtitle: Think about your actual daily work. There are no wrong answers.

questions.question: Which of these best describes a repetitive process in your work?
options: Processing incoming grant proposals (reading, extracting key details, filing)
Reviewing progress reports (checking completeness, summarizing findings)
Preparing compliance documentation (gathering data from multiple documents)
Routing and tracking subaward agreements or award modifications

questions.question: When you do this process manually, what takes the most time?
options: Reading through documents to find specific information
Re-typing data from documents into spreadsheets or forms
Comparing information across multiple documents
Writing summaries or reports based on document contents

questions.question: Which part of this process most requires your expertise and judgment?
options: Deciding whether extracted information meets compliance requirements
Interpreting ambiguous or unusual document content
Making recommendations based on institutional knowledge and context
All of the above — I need to review everything the AI produces

questions.question: If you could build a workflow for this process, what would the ideal output be?
options: A structured spreadsheet with extracted data from all documents
A summary report highlighting key findings and potential issues
A compliance checklist showing what's present and what's missing
A complete package ready for review (data + summary + checklist)

workflow_design.title: Workflow Design
workflow_design.subtitle: From Process Map to Pipeline Architecture
workflow_design.description: Now that you can decompose a process, learn how to translate it into a specific workflow architecture. Which task types fit which steps? How should data flow? Where do humans stay in the loop?
objectives: Map process steps to specific Vandalizer task types
Understand the extract-reason-deliver pattern
Design workflows that support human review, not replace it
tips: Start simple — a 2-3 step workflow that works is better than a 10-step workflow that doesn't
Design your output for the person who will review it, not for the computer
When in doubt about step granularity, split — it's easier to combine steps later than to debug one giant step

lessons.title: From process map to workflow architecture
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to map each step in a process to a specific Vandalizer task type.
lessons.content:
In Module 2, you decomposed a process into steps and identified which are AI-suitable vs. which stay human. Now you'll map each AI-suitable step to a specific Vandalizer task type — turning your process map into a buildable architecture.

The mapping is straightforward:
• "Find information in a document" → Extraction task with an Extraction
• "Analyze or summarize the extracted data" → Prompt task
• "Compute, total, or apply rules" → Code Execution task
• "Check a document for specific sections or elements" → Prompt task
• "Produce a formatted report or export" → Document Renderer or Data Export
• "Compare this document to another" → Add Document + Prompt task

The key insight: you're not starting from scratch asking "what can this tool do?" You're starting from your process and asking "which tool handles this step?"

lessons.title: The extract-reason-deliver pattern
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand the most reliable workflow pattern for research administration and why it works.
lessons.content:
The most common and most effective workflow pattern in research administration has three phases:

1. **Extract** — Pull structured data from the document. This is your Extraction step with a well-designed Extraction. The output is clean, consistent JSON.

2. **Reason** — Analyze the extracted data. This might be a Prompt step that summarizes findings, a Code Execution step that computes totals, or both. The output is analysis or computed results.

3. **Deliver** — Produce something useful. A formatted report, a CSV export, a compliance checklist, or structured data ready for your tracking system.

This pattern works because it separates concerns. If your final report is wrong, you can check: did the extraction get the right data? (Check step 1's output.) Did the analysis interpret it correctly? (Check step 2.) You can fix the broken step without rebuilding the whole workflow.

options.text: Analyzing the data to draw conclusions
options.explanation: Analysis is the "Reason" phase — it comes after extraction.

options.text: Producing a formatted report
options.explanation: Report generation is the "Deliver" phase — it comes last.

options.text: Pulling structured data from the document
options.explanation: Correct! Extract first, reason second, deliver third. Always start by getting clean data out of the document.

options.text: Asking the LLM to do everything in one prompt
options.explanation: One-shot prompting is the opposite of this pattern — it separates extraction from analysis from delivery.

lessons.title: Designing for your reviewer
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to design workflow output that makes human review fast rather than cumbersome.
lessons.content:
Here's a truth about AI in research administration: someone will always review the output. Maybe it's you, maybe it's a compliance officer, maybe it's a PI. Your workflow should make that review easy and efficient.

Design principles for reviewable output:

• **Show your sources** — When the workflow extracts a budget figure, the output should make it easy to verify against the source document.
• **Flag uncertainty** — If a field couldn't be found or the value seems unusual, the output should say so.
• **Structure for scanning** — The reviewer should be able to scan the output in 30 seconds and know if everything looks right.
• **Separate data from analysis** — Show the raw extracted data first, then the analysis or recommendations.

The workflow's job is not to eliminate review. It's to make review fast and focused.

lessons.title: Walkthrough: Designing a compliance review pipeline
lessons.content:
Process: "Check whether a grant proposal includes all required compliance elements."

**Step 1** — Decompose the process:
• Read the proposal and identify which compliance sections are present
• Extract specific compliance data (human subjects, data management, conflict of interest)
• Compare against the required elements checklist
• Produce a gap report showing what's present and what's missing

**Step 2** — Map to task types:
• "Read and extract compliance data" → Extraction task
• "Compare against checklist" → Prompt task
• "Produce gap report" → Document Renderer task

**Step 3** — Design data flow:
• Step 1 output: JSON with compliance field values (or "not found")
• Step 2 input: that JSON. Output: analysis text with present/missing/incomplete categories
• Step 3 input: analysis text. Output: formatted compliance checklist document

Result: A 3-step workflow. Upload a proposal, click Run, download a compliance checklist.

lessons.title: When to split, when to combine
lessons.content:
A common question: should this be one step or two?

**Split into separate steps when:**
• You'd want to check the intermediate output
• The operations are different types — extraction and analysis are different skills
• You might reuse one part
• Debugging would be easier

**Combine into one step when:**
• The operations are tightly coupled
• The intermediate output isn't useful on its own
• The combined prompt is simple and focused

Rule of thumb: start with more steps. You can always combine later once you know the workflow works. But splitting a monolithic step that's producing bad output is much harder than combining two steps that work well individually.

lessons.title: Chat-driven workflow design (v5.0)
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to design and dispatch workflows from conversation, using the agent as a design partner.
lessons.content:
In Vandalizer 5.0, you don't have to translate your process map into a workflow by yourself. The agent can help.

**Design in chat.** Describe the process in plain English: *"I need a 3-step workflow for subaward review. Step 1 extracts parties, amounts, and compliance terms. Step 2 analyzes key obligations. Step 3 formats a compliance summary."* The agent proposes an architecture with the right task types at each step. You refine it in the editor.

**Dispatch from chat.** Once built, you don't navigate to the workflow every time — you just ask: *"Run the subaward review on this agreement."* The agent picks the right workflow, streams each step as it runs, and shows you the output inline. Approval gates still pause for human review.

**Build faster with propose-then-refine.** For extractions specifically, you can say *"Propose an extraction set for NIH R01 proposals based on this document"* and the agent analyzes the document, suggests fields, and creates the template on confirmation. You save 20 minutes of field-by-field config.

The trade-off is still the same as any AI output: the agent's proposal is a starting point, not the final design. Use it to skip boilerplate, then apply your expertise to the parts that matter.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
These are the design decisions you'll face for every workflow you build. You've seen them applied in the compliance review walkthrough — use this as a reference when designing your own pipelines.

Step granularity — How many steps should your workflow have? Each step should do one clear thing. If you can't describe a step's purpose in one sentence, split it.

Task type selection — Choose the simplest task type that gets the job done. If you need structured data from a document, use Extraction — don't write a Prompt asking the LLM to produce JSON.

Data flow — Each step receives the previous step's output. Design your steps so the output of one naturally feeds the next. Extraction produces JSON; a Prompt can analyze that JSON; a Renderer can format the analysis.

Human checkpoints — Decide where a human should review before the workflow continues. In most research admin workflows, the answer is: review after the final output, not after every step.

Error tolerance — What happens if the LLM extracts a field incorrectly? Design your workflow so errors are visible in the output, not hidden. Show source data alongside conclusions.

assessment.title: Design Reflection
assessment.subtitle: Think about how you would structure workflows for your work.

questions.question: When you think about a process in your work, how would you decide where to split it into separate workflow steps?
options: Wherever I naturally take a break or hand off work to someone else
When the type of work changes (reading → analyzing → writing)
When I would want to verify output before proceeding to the next part
I'm not sure yet — I need to see more examples before I can decide

questions.question: For the documents your team processes, which workflow pattern seems most useful?
options: Extract then verify: pull structured data, then I review it
Extract, analyze, report: pull data, reason over it, produce a deliverable
Extract and compare: pull the same fields from multiple documents and compare them
Compliance pipeline: extract, check against rules, flag issues automatically

questions.question: What's your biggest concern about designing workflows for your team's processes?
options: I'm not sure how to break our processes into clear, discrete steps
I worry about choosing the wrong task types for each step
I'm concerned about maintaining and updating workflows as our processes change
I need help getting buy-in from my team to adopt workflow-based approaches

questions.question: When would you keep a step manual rather than adding it to the workflow?
options: When the step requires interpreting nuance or institutional context
When the stakes are too high for any AI error (regulatory compliance decisions)
When the input is too varied or unpredictable for consistent AI processing
All of the above — human judgment is irreplaceable for certain decisions

extraction_engine.title: Extraction Engine
extraction_engine.subtitle: Master the Extraction Pipeline
extraction_engine.description: Build a comprehensive 20+ field extraction using a sample NIH R01 proposal from Dr. James Park. The document has budget breakdowns, key personnel, and specific aims to extract.
objectives: Add the sample NIH R01 proposal to your workspace
Create an Extraction with 15+ fields covering all document sections
Extract budget categories, personnel, aims, and compliance fields
tips: The NIH R01 has clearly structured sections: budget, key personnel, specific aims, vertebrate animals
Use the Allowed values setting on a field to constrain answers to a list (e.g., Human Subjects: Yes/No, Clinical Trial: Yes/No)
Mark fields like Co-Investigator as optional since there may be multiple

lessons.title: One-pass vs. two-pass extraction
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know when to use two-pass vs. consensus extraction and what quality tradeoff you're making.
lessons.content:
Vandalizer offers two extraction strategies:

**One-pass extraction** sends the document and field definitions to the LLM in a single call. It's faster and cheaper, but can miss nuances in complex documents.

**Two-pass extraction** (the default) works in two stages:
• Pass 1: The LLM creates a draft extraction, thinking through each field.
• Pass 2: A second LLM call refines the draft, using structured output to produce clean, validated JSON.

The two-pass approach is more accurate because the second pass can correct mistakes from the first, and the structured output format prevents formatting errors.

knowledge_check.question: What makes two-pass extraction more accurate than one-pass?

options.text: It processes large documents by splitting them into chunks
options.explanation: That's chunking, a separate feature. Two-pass is about using a second LLM call to refine the first draft.

options.text: A second LLM call refines the first draft, correcting mistakes and enforcing structured output
options.explanation: Correct! The second pass reviews and cleans up the first pass, producing validated JSON with fewer errors.

options.text: It uses a smarter model for the second pass to catch errors from the first
options.explanation: Both passes use the same model. The improvement comes from the second pass having a draft to refine, not from a model change.

options.text: It runs the same extraction twice and averages the results
options.explanation: That's consensus repetition, not two-pass. Two-pass uses the first draft as context for a more accurate second attempt.

lessons.title: Configuring fields for accuracy
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to configure extraction fields that minimize hallucinations and missed values.
lessons.content:
The way you configure your Extraction fields directly impacts extraction quality:

**Field names** should be specific and unambiguous. "PI Name" is better than "Name". "Total Budget (USD)" is better than "Budget".

**Allowed values** (the field setting labeled "Allowed values" in the Extraction editor, sometimes called *enum values* in technical docs) constrain a field to a fixed list of options. For a field like "Document Type", you might set Allowed values to "Grant Proposal, Progress Report, Budget Justification". The LLM must pick one of those; it can't invent a new category. Use this any time the answer should be one of a known set: Yes/No, a status, a document type, a funding mechanism, etc.

**Optional fields** should be marked as such. If a field like "Co-PI" won't appear in every document, marking it optional tells the extraction engine not to hallucinate a value when one doesn't exist.

**Field descriptions** (in the title/searchphrase) give the LLM additional context about what to look for.

knowledge_check.question: When should you mark an Extraction field as optional?

options.text: When you're not sure what value to expect
options.explanation: Uncertainty about value isn't the reason to mark optional. Optional means the field might not exist in the document at all.

options.text: When the field may not appear in every document of that type
options.explanation: Correct! Marking a field optional tells the extraction engine not to hallucinate a value when the field simply isn't present.

options.text: When you want the LLM to skip the field to save processing time
options.explanation: Optional fields still get processed. The flag tells the engine it's acceptable to return null, not to skip the field.

options.text: When the field contains numbers instead of text
options.explanation: Data type doesn't determine optionality. A budget figure might always be present; that's not optional.

lessons.title: Build a comprehensive extraction
lessons.content:
1. Create a new Extraction or expand an existing one to 15+ fields.
2. Group related fields logically — e.g., personnel fields together, budget fields together.
3. For any categorical field (status, type, category), open the field and fill in the **Allowed values** setting with the list of valid options.
4. Mark fields that may not always be present as optional.
5. Add descriptive titles to help the LLM understand ambiguous fields.
6. Run the extraction on a test document and review the results.
7. Iterate: adjust field names and add Allowed values for any fields that extracted poorly.

lessons.title: When to use consensus repetition
lessons.content: Consensus repetition runs the same extraction 3 times and takes the majority answer for each field. Use it when the stakes are high — compliance data, financial figures, legal terms — and the cost of an incorrect extraction outweighs the 3x processing cost. For routine extractions or exploratory work, two-pass is usually sufficient.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Structured Output — You've now seen this in action: the LLM is constrained to return data matching a schema built from your Extraction fields. This is what prevents formatting errors and ensures you get the same JSON structure every time, regardless of how different the source documents look.

Thinking Mode — When enabled, the LLM reasons step-by-step before answering. Two-pass extraction uses Thinking Mode in Pass 1 for accuracy and disables it in Pass 2 for speed — you get both benefits.

Consensus Repetition — Runs the same extraction 3 times and takes the majority answer for each field. The right choice when stakes are high: compliance data, financial figures, legal terms. 3x the cost, but highest accuracy.

Chunking — When you have many fields (20+), the extraction splits into smaller batches to stay within the LLM's context window. Vandalizer handles this automatically when you exceed the threshold.

multi_step.title: Multi-Step Workflows
multi_step.subtitle: Chain Steps Together
multi_step.description: Build a multi-step pipeline using a sample subaward agreement between University of Idaho and Boise State. Extract parties and terms, analyze obligations, then format a compliance summary.
objectives: Add the sample subaward agreement to your workspace
Build a 3-step workflow: Extraction + Prompt + Formatter
Verify the pipeline chains correctly from extraction to formatted report
tips: The subaward has two parties (UI and BSU), financial terms, deliverables, and compliance requirements
Use the Prompt step to analyze obligations and flag key deadlines
The Formatter step should produce a clean compliance summary from the analysis

lessons.title: How steps chain together
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand how data flows between steps in a multi-step workflow.
lessons.content:
A multi-step workflow forms a pipeline where each step's output becomes the next step's input. The workflow engine executes steps in order (technically, in topological order of a directed acyclic graph, or DAG).

For example, a 3-step workflow might work like this:
• Step 1 (Extraction): Pulls structured fields from the document → outputs JSON.
• Step 2 (Prompt): Receives that JSON and asks the LLM to analyze it → outputs analysis text.
• Step 3 (Format): Takes the analysis and formats it into a clean report → outputs final document.

Each step can see the output of the step before it, creating a chain of increasingly refined output.

knowledge_check.question: What does a step receive as input by default?

options.text: The original uploaded document, regardless of step position
options.explanation: After step 1, the input source switches to the previous step's output, not the original document.

options.text: The output of the previous step
options.explanation: Correct! Each step's default input source is "step_input": the output of the step before it in the chain.

options.text: All outputs from every previous step, combined into one
options.explanation: Steps receive their immediate predecessor's output by default, not a combined history of all prior steps.

options.text: A fresh, empty context with only the system prompt
options.explanation: Steps are connected; each one builds on the previous step's work.

lessons.title: The Prompt node: reasoning over data
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know when to use an Extraction task vs. a Prompt task and why the distinction matters.
lessons.content:
The Prompt node is one of the most powerful tools in your workflow. It sends the previous step's output to the LLM along with your custom prompt, and returns the LLM's response.

Use it to:
• Summarize extracted data
• Compare and analyze
• Generate recommendations
• Transform formats

The key insight is that extraction gives you structured data, and prompts let you reason over that data.

knowledge_check.question: What's the key difference between an Extraction task and a Prompt task?

options.text: Extraction is faster; Prompt produces more detailed output
options.explanation: Speed isn't the defining difference. The distinction is about what kind of operation you're performing.

options.text: Extraction pulls structured fields from a document; Prompt reasons over data with a custom instruction
options.explanation: Correct! Use Extraction to get structured data out of a document. Use Prompt to analyze, summarize, or reason over that data.

options.text: Extraction only works on PDFs; Prompt works on any text format
options.explanation: Both task types work on text — the difference is in the operation, not the document format.

options.text: Extraction supports Thinking Mode; Prompt does not
options.explanation: Both task types can use Thinking Mode. The distinction is about extraction vs. reasoning, not about model settings.

lessons.title: Build a 3-step analysis workflow
lessons.content:
1. Create a new workflow and add 3 steps.
2. Step 1 — Add an Extraction task. Select an Extraction with fields relevant to your document.
3. Step 2 — Add a Prompt task. Write a prompt that analyzes the extracted data.
4. Step 3 — Add a Formatter task. Write a template that structures the final output.
5. Select a document and run the workflow. Observe how data flows through each step.
6. Review each step's output individually using the step-by-step output panel.

lessons.title: Design principle: extract first, reason second
lessons.content:
*You've seen this principle before in Module 3 — that's intentional. Spaced repetition builds retention. Let's revisit it and see how it applies when chaining multiple steps.*

A common mistake is trying to do everything in one big prompt. Instead, separate extraction (getting facts from documents) from reasoning (drawing conclusions from those facts). This makes each step simpler, more reliable, and easier to debug. If your final output is wrong, you can check: did the extraction step get the right data? Or did the prompt step misinterpret it? This separation is what makes workflows more reliable than one-shot prompting.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Input Source — Controls what data a step receives. You've now seen "step_input" in action — the default that passes each step's output to the next. Other options let you inject a specific document or all workflow documents at any point in the chain.

Prompt Node — The reasoning engine in your pipeline. It takes the previous step's output and your custom instruction, and returns the LLM's analysis. You used it to transform extracted JSON into a compliance summary.

Format Node — Transforms structured data into formatted text (markdown, plain text). Use it when you want a human-readable report from raw extracted data without writing a custom prompt.

Post-process Prompt — An optional final LLM call on any node's output. Use it to clean up or lightly reformat results without adding a full extra step to your pipeline.

advanced_nodes.title: Advanced Nodes
advanced_nodes.subtitle: Parallel Tasks & Power Nodes
advanced_nodes.description: Process a sample budget justification document using an advanced node (the Research node) to analyze the figures, plus parallel tasks for concurrent processing.
objectives: Add the sample budget justification to your workspace
Use an advanced node (Research, API, or Crawler) to analyze the budget
Run 2+ tasks in parallel within a single step
tips: The budget has personnel costs, supplies, travel, and subaward line items that should sum to $542,800
Use the Research node to analyze the extracted figures and check whether the line items add up. It runs two LLM passes over the data, no URL or API key required
Add a parallel Prompt task alongside the Research node to generate a budget narrative

lessons.title: Beyond extraction and prompts
lessons.content:
Vandalizer has 17 different node types. So far you've used Extraction, Prompt, and Format — but the advanced nodes let you go much further:

• **Research** — Two-pass analysis: first analyzes the data, then synthesizes findings into a report. Works on your document — no URL or API key needed.
• **API Call** — Make HTTP requests to external services.
• **Crawler** — Fetch and extract text from websites.
• **Add Document / Add Website** — Inject additional context mid-workflow.
• **Code Execution** — Run sandboxed Python for calculations or custom logic. This is an admin-only power node and is not in the standard palette, so you won't use it in this challenge.

lessons.title: Code Execution: custom logic in your pipeline
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know which kinds of logic belong in Code Execution rather than Prompt nodes.
lessons.content:
The Code Execution node lets you write Python that runs inside your workflow. It is an **admin-only power node** that is not in the standard palette — you won't add it in this module, but it's worth knowing when it's the right tool. It is powerful for:

• **Data transformation** — Normalize dates, convert currencies, merge fields.
• **Calculations** — Compute totals, percentages, or ratios from extracted numbers.
• **Filtering** — Remove irrelevant results or flag outliers.
• **Format conversion** — Reshape JSON into a different structure.

The code runs in a sandbox: no file system access, no network access, no imports beyond the standard library. For document analysis without code — like checking whether a budget adds up — the Research node is the everyday equivalent.

knowledge_check.question: Which task should use Code Execution instead of a Prompt node?

options.text: Extracting structured fields from a grant proposal
options.explanation: That's what Extraction tasks are for. Code Execution is for logic that requires precision, not pattern recognition.

options.text: Summarizing a progress report into bullet points
options.explanation: Summarization is a Prompt task — it's language work that LLMs do well.

options.text: Adding up budget line items or computing percentages from extracted numbers
options.explanation: Correct! Math requires precision. LLMs frequently make arithmetic errors, so use Code Execution for any numerical computation.

options.text: Comparing two documents and identifying differences
options.explanation: Document comparison is a Prompt task — it involves language understanding, which is LLM territory.

lessons.title: Running tasks in parallel
lessons.content:
Within a single step, you can add multiple tasks. These tasks run concurrently — the workflow engine starts them all at the same time and waits for them to finish together, instead of running them one after another.

This is useful when you need multiple independent operations:
• Extract from two different Extractions at the same time
• Call multiple APIs in parallel
• Run an extraction while simultaneously fetching enrichment data from a website

To add parallel tasks, open a step in the workflow editor and click "Add Task" multiple times.

knowledge_check.question: When you add multiple tasks to a single step, how do they run?

options.text: One at a time, in the order you added them
options.explanation: Sequential execution would defeat the purpose of parallel tasks. Vandalizer runs them concurrently.

options.text: Concurrently, they all run at the same time
options.explanation: Correct! Multiple tasks within a step run in parallel: they all start at roughly the same time, and the step waits for them all to finish. Their results are collected and passed to the next step.

options.text: In a random order determined by server load
options.explanation: Tasks run concurrently, not in a random order. All start at roughly the same time.

options.text: Only the first task runs; the rest are treated as fallbacks if it fails
options.explanation: All tasks run. There's no fallback logic between tasks in a step.

lessons.title: Build a workflow with advanced nodes
lessons.content:
1. Create a workflow with at least 3 steps.
2. In one step, add a **Research** task. Give it a question like "Do these budget line items add up to the stated total?" and point its input at the document or the previous step's output.
3. In the same step, add a second task (for example a Prompt task that writes a narrative summary) so two tasks run in parallel.
4. Run the workflow and review how the parallel tasks' outputs are combined.
5. (Optional) Swap the Research node for an API Call or Crawler node to see other advanced node types.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Parallel Tasks — You've now run multiple tasks within a single step concurrently. Their results are collected and passed to the next step together. The benefit: independent operations (two extractions, or an extraction + API call) happen simultaneously instead of sequentially.

Code Execution — Python in a restricted sandbox with a 10-second timeout. The previous step's output is available as `input_data`. Assign your result to `output`. Use for any math, date calculations, or deterministic logic — never for work that needs language understanding.

API Call — Connects your workflow to external services. Supports GET, POST, PUT, and PATCH. You can pass authentication headers and use the previous step's output in the request body — enabling real-time lookups and integrations.

Research Node — Two-stage analysis: first passes through the data to identify patterns, then synthesizes findings into a coherent report. Use when you need more depth than a single Prompt call provides.

output_delivery.title: Output & Delivery
output_delivery.subtitle: Produce Real Deliverables
output_delivery.description: Process a sample Year-2 progress report and produce downloadable deliverables. Extract accomplishments, publications, and budget data, then export as a report or CSV.
objectives: Add the sample progress report to your workspace
Create a workflow with an output node (Document Renderer, Data Export, etc.)
Run the workflow and download the generated output file
tips: The progress report has publications, students trained, and budget expenditures to extract
Document Renderer is great for producing a formatted summary report
Data Export with CSV format works well for the budget expenditure data

lessons.title: From analysis to deliverables
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know which output node to use for different types of deliverables.
lessons.content:
So far, your workflows produce text output that you view in the app. But real research administration often requires deliverables: compliance reports to submit, data exports for spreadsheets, or document packages with multiple files.

Vandalizer's output nodes transform your workflow results into downloadable files:

• **Document Renderer** — Generates a markdown or text file from your workflow output.
• **Data Export** — Exports structured data as JSON or CSV.
• **Package Builder** — Creates a ZIP archive containing multiple output files.
• **Form Filler** — Takes a template with placeholders and fills it with extracted data.

knowledge_check.question: You need to export extracted data from 50 proposals into a spreadsheet. Which output node is the right choice?

options.text: Document Renderer — it produces a formatted text file from workflow output
options.explanation: Document Renderer creates a readable document, not structured tabular data. It's better for reports you'd read, not data you'd analyze in Excel.

options.text: Package Builder — it bundles multiple files into a ZIP
options.explanation: Package Builder is for collecting multiple output files together, not for producing spreadsheet-compatible data.

options.text: Data Export — it converts structured data to CSV or JSON
options.explanation: Correct! Data Export with CSV format turns your extracted JSON into columns and rows that open directly in Excel or Google Sheets.

options.text: Form Filler — it fills placeholders in a template with extracted values
options.explanation: Form Filler is for template-based documents (like filling out a standard form), not for exporting tabular data.

lessons.title: Designing end-to-end deliverable workflows
lessons.content:
The most powerful workflows go from raw document to finished deliverable in one run:

1. **Extract** — Pull structured data from the source document.
2. **Analyze** — Use Prompt nodes to reason over the data, flag issues, or generate summaries.
3. **Render** — Use output nodes to produce the final deliverable.

The result: upload a grant proposal, click Run, and download a completed compliance checklist.

knowledge_check.question: What are the three phases of a complete end-to-end deliverable workflow?

options.text: Upload, Run, Download
options.explanation: Those are UI actions, not the workflow's internal phases. The phases describe what the steps do, not what the user does.

options.text: Extract, Analyze, Render
options.explanation: Correct! Extract structured data, analyze or reason over it, then render a deliverable. This maps to the task types: Extraction → Prompt → Output node.

options.text: Prompt, Format, Export
options.explanation: Those are task type names, not the three-phase pattern. The pattern is higher-level: Extract → Analyze → Render.

options.text: Parse, Validate, Deliver
options.explanation: Parse and Validate aren't workflow phases in Vandalizer. The pattern is Extract (get data), Analyze (reason about it), Render (produce output).

lessons.title: Build a deliverable workflow
lessons.content:
1. Start with a workflow that extracts and analyzes data (from Module 3).
2. Add a new step at the end of your workflow.
3. Add a Document Renderer or Data Export task to that step.
4. For Document Renderer: the previous step's output will be rendered as a downloadable file.
5. For Data Export: choose JSON or CSV format.
6. Run the workflow on a document.
7. In the results panel, you'll see a download link for the generated file.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Document Renderer — You've now generated downloadable files from workflow output. Document Renderer takes the previous step's text and wraps it into a file. Best for reports, summaries, and compliance checklists that people will read.

Data Export — Converts structured JSON data into CSV or JSON files. When using CSV, each extracted key becomes a column header. Best for data that will be loaded into spreadsheets, databases, or other systems.

Package Builder — Collects outputs from multiple steps and bundles them into a single ZIP file. Use it when your workflow produces several distinct deliverables that should be distributed together.

Form Filler — Takes a template string with placeholder syntax and produces a filled-in version using your extracted data. Best when the output must follow a fixed format (like a standard institutional form).

Include in deliverables — A per-step toggle that marks the step's output as part of the downloadable result. Mark multiple steps to bundle their outputs as a ZIP. If no step is marked, the last step is used by default. Use it to control which steps are deliverables vs. intermediate processing steps.

validation_qa.title: Validation & QA
validation_qa.subtitle: Ensure Quality at Scale
validation_qa.description: Add validation to your NSF proposal workflow from Module 1. Define quality checks that verify your extraction produces correct results, then run validation to measure accuracy.
objectives: Open your workflow from Module 1 (or create a new one for the NSF proposal)
Create a validation plan with 2+ quality checks
Run validation and review the results
tips: This module reuses the NSF proposal from Module 1 - no new documents needed
Start with checks like "PI Name is not null" and "Total Budget is a valid number"
Use auto-generated validation checks as a starting point, then customize

lessons.title: Why validation matters
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand why validation is essential before deploying a workflow for production use.
lessons.content:
An extraction workflow that works on one document might fail on the next. Different document layouts, writing styles, or terminology can cause the LLM to miss fields or return incorrect values.

Validation lets you define what "correct" looks like for your workflow, test it against sample documents, and track reliability over time.

knowledge_check.question: What is the primary purpose of a validation plan?

options.text: To prevent users from accidentally running a workflow on the wrong document type
options.explanation: That's a permissions or UI concern. Validation plans are about output quality, not access control.

options.text: To define what correct output looks like, test it against samples, and track reliability over time
options.explanation: Correct! A validation plan defines correctness, tests against known documents, and builds a quality history you can track over time.

options.text: To automatically fix extraction errors before they reach the user
options.explanation: Validation detects problems — it doesn't fix them. You use the results to improve your extraction configuration.

options.text: To comply with institutional data governance requirements
options.explanation: Validation is about workflow quality, not regulatory compliance. It helps you trust your workflow's output.

lessons.title: Building effective validation plans
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to design a validation plan that catches the most important failure modes.
lessons.content:
Good validation plans check multiple dimensions of quality:

• **Completeness** — Did the workflow extract all expected fields?
• **Accuracy** — Do extracted values match the known-correct values?
• **Format** — Are dates in the right format? Are numbers parsed correctly?
• **Consistency** — When run multiple times, does the workflow produce the same results?

Start with 2-3 high-value checks and expand as you gain confidence.

knowledge_check.question: A good validation plan checks two foundational dimensions of quality. What are they?

options.text: Speed (tokens per second) and cost (dollars per run)
options.explanation: Those are performance metrics, not quality dimensions. A fast, cheap workflow that extracts the wrong data is useless.

options.text: Completeness (all fields extracted) and accuracy (values are correct)
options.explanation: Correct! Completeness checks that nothing was missed. Accuracy checks that what was extracted is actually right.

options.text: Model version and prompt version
options.explanation: Those are configuration metadata. Quality checks what the workflow actually produces, not what configuration it uses.

options.text: Document count and processing time
options.explanation: Those are throughput metrics. Validation is about output quality, not volume or speed.

lessons.title: Set up validation for your workflow
lessons.content:
1. Open your workflow in the editor and go to the Validate tab.
2. Add validation inputs — paste sample text or select documents.
3. Create a validation plan with at least 2 quality checks.
4. Run validation. The system executes your workflow and grades the results.
5. Review the results: which checks passed, which failed, and why.
6. Use improvement suggestions to iterate on your extraction.
7. Check quality history to see how your workflow improves over time.

lessons.title: Validation as a safety net
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand when to re-run validation and why it should be set up before a workflow goes into production.
lessons.content: The best time to set up validation is before you need it. When you change your Extraction fields, update a prompt, or when the underlying LLM model is updated, your workflow's behavior might change. If you have a validation plan, you can re-run it immediately to check for regressions.

lessons.title: Trust signals & quality tiers (v5.0)
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll be able to read a quality badge in chat and know what each score, tier, and alert means.
lessons.content:
In Vandalizer 5.0, every validated template carries a trust signal that shows up inline in chat replies. Learning to read it is the difference between trusting an answer and second-guessing it.

The badge encodes four things:

• **Tier (color)** — `excellent` (green, 90+), `good` (blue, 75–89), `fair` (yellow, 50–74), `poor` (red, <50). Tiers are thresholds over the unified score.
• **Score (0–100)** — A weighted combination of accuracy (does the extraction match ground truth?) and consistency (do repeated runs produce identical values?).
• **Grade** — Letter grade assigned at the most recent ValidationRun.
• **Active alerts** — Unacknowledged QualityAlerts for the template. A critical alert means accuracy dropped below floor; a warning typically means the score is stale.

Hover the badge to see the full breakdown: accuracy %, consistency %, test-case count, and last validation date.

**What makes a score rise:** more test cases (broader ground truth), fewer mismatches with ground truth (tighter prompts, better field names).

**What makes a score drop:** consistency failures (same extraction returning different values across runs) or accuracy regressions after a model/prompt change.

**What the LLM can and can't do:** The LLM is told that a result is validated, but it cannot see or fabricate the actual score. The number on the badge comes from stored ValidationRun records, not the model's opinion.

lessons.title: Guided verification (v5.0)
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll know how to promote a good extraction into a test case from chat, using the guided verification modal.
lessons.content:
Good test cases are the fuel for every quality score. In 5.0, building them is a conversation.

**The flow:**
1. You run an extraction that looks right — say *"Extract PI and budget from this proposal."*
2. You tell the agent to promote it: *"Save this as a test case."*
3. The agent calls `propose_test_case`, which returns a VerificationSession and opens the guided verification modal in the document viewer.
4. The modal shows each extracted field with its value and a link back to the document passage. You confirm each value or correct it in place.
5. On save, a persisted test case joins the ground-truth set. Next validation run uses it.

**Why it matters:** Without guided verification, building test cases requires opening every document manually and transcribing correct values — a chore nobody does. With it, every good extraction you already ran becomes a test case in under a minute. The trust tier rises as a side effect of normal work.

**When to use it:**
• An unvalidated template returns a clean result — lock it in as the first test case.
• A template in the `fair` tier has a weak field — add test cases that specifically exercise that field on documents where it's clear.
• You're training a new template on a new domain — 3 to 5 test cases from different documents unlock a meaningful score.

**What to watch for:** If the agent proposes a test case and several fields are wrong, don't just correct them silently. Ask *"Why did these fields fail?"* — the agent will flag common issues (document format, ambiguous field names, etc.) you can fix in the template itself.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Validation Plan — The definition of "correct" for your workflow. You've now built one: a list of checks that specify what good output looks like. This is what separates a workflow you trust from one you hope works.

Validation Input — The sample documents or text you test your workflow against. Good validation inputs are representative — they should reflect the range of documents the workflow will encounter in production.

Validation Run — An execution of your workflow against validation inputs, automatically graded against your plan. Each run adds a data point to your quality history.

Quality History — A log of validation run scores over time. This is how you detect regressions: if a score drops after you change a field name or update a model, quality history shows you exactly when.

Improvement Suggestions — LLM-generated recommendations for improving extraction accuracy based on which checks failed and why. A starting point for iteration, not a final answer.

batch_processing.title: Batch Processing
batch_processing.subtitle: Process at Scale
batch_processing.description: Process three sample NSF proposals in batch mode. Each proposal is from a different PI (Lopez, Kim, Okafor) with different research areas and budgets.
objectives: Add 3 sample batch proposals to your workspace
Run a workflow in batch mode against all 3 documents
Verify all 3 complete successfully with correct PI names
tips: Use your extraction workflow from Module 1 or 2, or create a new one
The three proposals have PIs: Dr. Maria Lopez, Dr. Robert Kim, Dr. Amara Okafor
Check that all 3 documents complete successfully before marking done

lessons.title: Single vs. batch execution
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand when and how to use batch mode for large-scale document processing.
lessons.content:
So far you've been running workflows on one document at a time. Batch mode lets you process multiple documents in a single operation.

In batch mode, the workflow runs once per document, sequentially. Each document gets its own WorkflowResult, and you can monitor progress for the entire batch.

This is the core value proposition of Vandalizer: define a workflow once, validate it, then run it across hundreds of documents with confidence.

knowledge_check.question: What does batch mode do that single-document mode doesn't?

options.text: Uses a more powerful model to produce higher-quality results
options.explanation: Batch mode doesn't change the model. You can choose any model in either mode.

options.text: Runs the workflow once per selected document, giving each its own result
options.explanation: Correct! Batch mode queues one workflow execution per document. Each run is independent with its own WorkflowResult you can review separately.

options.text: Skips validation checks to process documents faster
options.explanation: Batch mode doesn't skip validation. It just applies the workflow across multiple documents automatically.

options.text: Merges the output of all documents into a single combined result
options.explanation: Each document gets its own result. Merging outputs is a workflow design choice, not something batch mode does automatically.

lessons.title: Monitoring and debugging batch runs
lessons.content:
When running a batch:

• **Real-time progress** — The UI shows which document is currently processing.
• **Per-document results** — Each document's result is stored independently.
• **Error handling** — Common failures include documents that are too long, unexpected formats, or missing expected fields.

Always test your workflow on a single document before running a batch.

knowledge_check.question: What should you always do before running a batch?

options.text: Export your workflow as a .vandalizer.json file for backup
options.explanation: Exporting is useful for sharing, but not a prerequisite for batch runs.

options.text: Increase the model's context window to handle larger documents
options.explanation: Context window is a model property you can't configure directly. The right prep step is validating your workflow works on a sample.

options.text: Test the workflow on a single document first to confirm it works
options.explanation: Correct! Always validate with a single document before scaling up. Debugging a batch of 200 failed runs is much harder than fixing one.

options.text: Create a validation plan with at least 5 checks
options.explanation: A validation plan is a good practice, but the essential step is a single-document test run to confirm the workflow produces usable output.

lessons.title: Choosing the right model for batch work
lessons.content:
Model selection matters more for batch processing because costs and time multiply across documents. Consider speed, cost, accuracy, and data privacy tradeoffs.

You can override the model per-workflow or per-task. Consider using a faster model for format/prompt steps and a more capable model for extraction steps.

lessons.title: Run your first batch
lessons.content:
1. Ensure you have a workflow that works reliably on a single document.
2. Upload at least 3 documents of the same type to your workspace.
3. Select all 3 documents, then open your workflow.
4. Choose "Batch" mode.
5. Start the batch. Watch the real-time progress.
6. When complete, review the results for each document.
7. If any failed, inspect the error, fix the issue, and re-run just the failed documents.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Batch Mode — You've now run a workflow across multiple documents in one operation. In batch mode, the workflow executes once per document — each run independent, each result stored separately. This is the core of what makes Vandalizer useful at scale.

Batch ID — A unique identifier for the batch run. All documents processed in the same batch share this ID, making it easy to find and review the full set of results.

Session ID — Each individual document execution within a batch has its own session ID. Use it when you need to review or debug a specific document's result.

Batch Status — The aggregated view: how many documents completed successfully, how many failed, and how many are still in progress. Check this to know when your batch is done and whether anything needs reprocessing.

governance.title: Collaboration & Governance
governance.subtitle: Share and Standardize
governance.description: The final module before your Vandal Workflow Architect certification. Demonstrate that you can organize, verify, and share production-ready workflows across your team. Complete this and you earn your VWA credential.
objectives: Mark a workflow as verified in the workflow settings
Use workflows across personal and team contexts
No new documents needed - uses workflows you have already built
tips: Switch into a shared team if you want to practice collaboration flows
Export workflows as .vandalizer.json files to share with teammates
Verified workflows signal to your team that a workflow is production-ready

lessons.title: Organizing for reuse
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand the three tiers of workflow organization and when to use each.
lessons.content:
As your team builds more workflows, organization becomes critical. Use personal work for drafting, then move the workflows your team should reuse into shared team libraries and verified collections.

Think about organization in terms of ownership and audience:
• **Personal work** — early drafts, experiments, and one-off variations.
• **Team libraries** — shared workflows your group actively maintains.
• **Verified collections** — approved workflows that set team standards.

lessons.title: The verification workflow
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you'll understand what "verified" communicates to your team and how to use it as a governance tool.
lessons.content:
Marking a workflow as "verified" is a governance practice. It signals to your team that:

1. The workflow has been tested on representative documents.
2. A validation plan exists and passes consistently.
3. The output format meets the team's requirements.
4. The workflow is ready for production use.

Verification isn't a technical gate — it's a team communication tool.

knowledge_check.question: What does marking a workflow as "verified" communicate to your team?

options.text: The workflow is locked and cannot be edited by other team members
options.explanation: Verified is not a lock. It's a signal about quality, not a permission restriction.

options.text: The workflow has been tested, validated, and is approved for production use
options.explanation: Correct! Verified is a team communication tool. It says: this workflow has been through quality checks and is ready to rely on.

options.text: The workflow was created or approved by an admin-level user
options.explanation: Any team member can mark a workflow verified. It's about the workflow's quality, not the creator's role.

options.text: The workflow only uses LLM models approved by your institution
options.explanation: Model approval is a separate concern. Verified is about whether the workflow's output meets your team's quality bar.

lessons.title: Sharing workflows across teams
lessons.content:
Workflows can be shared in two ways:

• **Within the same team** — Duplicate or adapt workflows inside the team workspace and library.
• **Cross-team sharing via export/import** — Export a workflow as a .vandalizer.json file. Send it to a colleague, who can import it.

Sharing verified workflows establishes organizational standards.

lessons.title: Establish your workflow governance
lessons.content:
1. Pick a workflow that is ready to share beyond your personal work.
2. Build or duplicate that workflow into the team context where others should reuse it.
3. Make sure your workflow has a clear description.
4. If you completed Module 8, ensure your validation plan passes.
5. Mark the workflow as verified in the workflow settings.
6. Try exporting and importing the workflow.
7. You now have a verified, portable, well-documented workflow.

lessons.title: Building a culture of reuse
lessons.content:
The highest-performing teams maintain a library of verified workflows that cover common document types, then adapt and extend them as needed.

By completing this module, you've demonstrated every skill in the Vandal Workflow Architect program: understanding AI, decomposing processes, designing pipelines, building extractions, chaining multi-step workflows, using advanced nodes, producing deliverables, validating quality, processing at scale, and governing shared workflows.

You're now a certified VWA — the person on your team who knows how to turn any document-heavy process into a reliable, AI-powered pipeline. That's a rare and valuable skill.

knowledge_check.question: A colleague asks how to verify that budget totals are correct in a workflow. What's the right approach?

options.text: Write a Prompt node that instructs the LLM to add up the extracted numbers
options.explanation: LLMs frequently make arithmetic errors. Never rely on a Prompt node for math — that's Module 2's key lesson.

options.text: Use consensus repetition so three extractions vote on the correct total
options.explanation: Consensus repetition improves extraction accuracy, but it won't fix arithmetic — three LLMs making the same math error still agree.

options.text: Use a Code Execution node after extraction — code is reliable for math, LLMs aren't
options.explanation: Correct! This applies the core principle from Module 3: use code for computation, AI for language. Code always gets math right.

options.text: Extract the numbers and manually verify them after the workflow runs
options.explanation: Manual verification defeats the purpose of a workflow. Building the check into the pipeline is the right architectural move.

lessons.title: Glossary & Review
lessons.content:
Personal work — Workflows and resources that only you can see and edit. The right place for experiments, drafts, and one-off variations. Graduate your best work to the team context when it's ready to share.

Verified — A governance flag that communicates quality. You've now marked a workflow verified — that signal tells your team the workflow has been tested, validated, and approved for production use. It's a team communication tool, not a technical lock.

Export (.vandalizer.json) — A portable file containing your workflow's complete definition: steps, tasks, field configurations, prompts. It can be imported into any Vandalizer instance and is the standard format for cross-team sharing.

Team — A group of users who share access to team workflows, libraries, and folders. Members have roles: owner, admin, or member. Verified workflows in the team context become the standards your whole team builds on.
